Initially, this test continued to fail even after I'd implemented the functionality.

The test case is where the session includes a specific value for `verify`, but the user doesn't directly specify a value in the `HttSleeper` API.  Unfortunately, the session value was being ignored, and `verify=True` was being used instead, running counter to the standard behaviour of the `requests` library.

Thus I was forced to revisit the discussion we had in https://github.com/kopf/httsleep/pull/12#discussion_r145198802 about whether `HttSleeper` should by default send `verify=True` or leave the kwarg unspecified.  That's why my 4th commit changes this behaviour back to that which I originally proposed in the last PR 😃

It won't get caught in Tornado but it'll be raised in an unexpected context. `fetch` returns a future and exceptions for that operation are expected inside that future. If this was just a simple `raise`, the call to fetch will throw an exception instead of the future. It will also not respect the `raise_error` flag if `raise`d directly.

BTW, there was a bug here because the exception wasn't actually being raised (I forgot to call the callback). Fix and more tests in #165.

Unfortunately you can't safely install a signal handler in Pythons prior to 3.5!  Most signals in Python retain their default disposition, which mostly (again) is to terminate the program (see man 7 signal) or to ignore the signal.

Consequently very little Python code that makes system calls is prepared to handle EINTR. When you set your own handler for some signal, that signal will now cause system calls to terminate with an EINTR error. These two facts together mean that registering signal handlers in Python is serious business!  

For example, using this code will mean that if you have a `time.sleep` or a `socket.recv` in your main thread, resizing the terminal will cause that call to fail with EINTR!

Python 3.5 attempts to resolve this: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0475/  In prior versions it's better to either not set signal handlers at all, or to only set them as part of some larger event handling system (as Twisted does).

See, `def` in a trait without implementation is just a general idea of something being provided in implementations. The implementation may be def, or val, or lazy val. Def will reevaluate on every reference, which makes no sense. So either val or lazy val. I made implementation lazy val for the reason of avoiding bad initialization order. Scala people blame JVM, but I would blame scala compiler. It happens pretty frequently that a member of a class refers another member, and if that member is not initialized yet (just because it's below, textually), it is only discovered in runtime, and it is null. Not sure if there is a bug filed, but well, our situation is special, we are targeting a range of compilers, as I understand.

Just reran Jim's scripts.

The first result is that switching from the arbitrary 50%-of-threshold algorithm for edge clipping to the algorithm suggested above yields 2x improvement in photometric accuracy at essentially negligible difference in speed.  (Still not as good as preserving the range though).

More interesting though are the comparisons with the fixed version of the old algorithm and the new algorithm.  Here's the fixed old algorithm with preserve_range=False:

![fixed_color_comp](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/14686891/a4dcf2bc-06ef-11e6-8044-20f7efa0ed3f.png)

Here's the fixed old algorithm with preserve_range=True:

![fixed_preserve_range_color_comp](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/14687225/4142b55a-06f1-11e6-853a-8258d56c9df1.png)

This one surprised me (checked it twice!)  My best guess is that by proceeding blue-to-red, there's a bias in the way points get subtracted from the wave/flux lists.  I.e., you'd get a different result if you reversed the order of the input wave and flux lists.  The new algorithm, by contrast, is symmetric.  For reference, here's the results with preserve_range=True:

![new_new_preserve_range_color_comp](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/14687425/4c63900c-06f2-11e6-839c-d1ea27cc90d9.png)


So anyway, I think my proposal is to make the new algorithm with preserve_range=True the default.

>maybe it would be good if the tx_validation_fn had a different signature that provided this contextual information

I'm not too crazy about that tbh because that seems too much of an implementation detail of a specific validator to me. In general, I'd like to keep the validator fn `Callable[[tx], bool]` because that really seems like the most common interface that all possible validators would share. E.g. imagine you want a validator that takes a proof of authority approach. It wouldn't care about the block number and maybe not even about the chain but instead check if the tx sender is part of some predefined list.

Of course, we could say such a validator may just ignore `chain` or `block_number` as superfluous contextual information but it feels like a smell to me that we don't want it in the interface at all.

The synopses in this document (`records.rst`) use a different voice than those in the other documents (that I've reviewed so far, at least). Consider the following:
> Stores a record in the collection.
vs.
Store a record in a collection.

The first statement is in an active voice ([imperative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperative_mood)) whereas the second is passive. It's a subtle distinction, and I'm not arguing for one or the other - only that we be consistent across the documentation.

In other words, the form of the synopses in this document should be changed to match that of the other documents. :smile:

This method definitely needs some renaming (and doc-updating); it should be "LastUpdatedVersion" or something like that. The name here comes from when I was originally planning to have the ONNX repo continuously keep all information about operators, both old ones (who have since been updated in a BC-breaking way) and the up-to-date ones.

Don and I have discussed a bit about how exactly want to go about representing history change in defs. A more traditional model is that the state of the Git repository at any point in time is the definitive description of the operator state at this point in time. So, for example, if the current ONNX operator version is 34 and you want 12, you'll have to check out the ONNX repo as it was at that version to get the canonical description of that ONNX operator state. When the only description of operators is C++ code, this is not a great state of affairs; so an important further component of this story is a normative protobuf description of the operators at some version, made available for download in some way. The good thing about this approach is that it closely matches ordinary software development practice, and it doesn't require us to change very much our model for operator development; plus, you can't "edit" history.

Alternate solutions to handling `nrows`:
1. Read file line-by-line. Requires more code, but `read_csv` will still be similar speed to Pandas because it requires reading all rows sequentially. Slight optimization when `nrows` is small.
2. Use current read_csv method and drop unneeded rows. Much slower than Pandas when `nrows` is small and the file is large, but could be faster when `nrows` is large and the file is large.
